Facts.text: Magister militum Ricimer defeats the Emperor Avitus at Piacenza and becomes master of the western Roman Empire
Council of Vienne (15th ecumenical council) opens
Jadwiga is crowned King of Poland aged nine, despite being female
Milan Cathedral's high altar consecrated by Pope Martin V
Storm ravages Friese coast
English Edward Seymour Duke of Somerset and former Lord Protector, re-arrested for treason - executed three months later
Olivier van Noort's Dutch ships reach the Philippines during their circumnavigation of the world
Emperor Leopold I fires chancellor Fürst Wenzel Lobkowitz
British troops occupies Port Royal, Nova Scotia
Austrian troops occupy Berlin
Portland, Maine burned by British
Royalton, Vermont and Tunbridge, Vermont last major raid of the American Revolutionary War
Battle of Wattignies: French defeat Allied forces and lift siege of Maubeuge
M von Böhms "Oorlogscantate" premieres
Tremont Hotel, 1st US modern hotel opens (Boston)
Much of the ancient structures of the Palace of Westminster (parliament) in London is burnt down
Joseph Saxton, a machine inspector at the US Mint, takes 1st daguerrotype photograph in US, of Central High School building in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Queens University in Kingston, Ontario, Canada, is chartered
Sir William Rowan Hamilton comes up with the idea of quaternions, a non-commutative extension of complex numbers.
Dentist William T Morton demonstrates effectiveness of ether
1st US homeopathic medical college opens in Pennsylvania
Avery College establishes in Allegheny, Pennsylvania
British seize Tigre Island in Gulf of Fonseca from Honduras
Dutch government recognize Catholics right to organize
Confederacy starts selling postage stamps
Grant is given command of Union forces in West
Hotel in Boston becomes 1st to have indoor plumbing
1st Quebec vs Ontario football game, Ontario wins
Race riot at Cainhoy SC (5 whites & 1 black killed)
The Nickel Plate Railroad opens for business.
Great Britain and Germany sign the Anglo-German Treaty, agreeing to maintain the territorial integrity of China and support the 'open door' policy called for by the US Secretary of State
Baron Hayashi of Japan begins negotiations in London to make an alliance with the British and strengthen Japan's position against Russians
Homel, 1st Jewish self defense organization founded in Russia
Russian Baltic fleet departs to Port Arthur
The Partition of Bengal (India) announced by Lord Curzon
Belasco Theater opens at 111 W 44th St NYC
David Belasco's "Grand Army Man," premieres in NYC
Edmonton Rugby Foot-ball Club re-organizes as Esquimoux
Booth Theater opens at 222 W 44th St NYC
Great Britain declares war on Bulgaria
Jim Conzelman takes over as coach of Rock Island Independents from Frank Coughlin-only mid-game coaching change in NFL history
Disney Brothers Cartoon Studio is founded
John Harwood patents self-winding watch (Switzerland)
Peace accord of Locarno signed (Rhine Pact)
Texas School Board prohibits teaching of evolution
Mohammed Nadir Khan begins coup in Afghanistan, 1200 killed
Troop ship sinks in Yangtze River, killing 1,200
US trunk murderess Winnie Ruth Judd murders two friends then dismembers one of them in Phoenix
Sugar rationing begins in the Netherlands
Benjamin Oliver Davis Sr. is promoted to brigadier general, the first African-American person to become a general in the US military
"Gordo" comic strip (by Gus Arriola) 1st appears in newspapers
Germany advances within 60 miles (96 K) of Moscow
Aaron Coplandand Agnes de Mille's ballet "Rodeo," premieres in NYC
Cyclone in Bay of Bengal kills some 40,000 south of Calcutta India
National Boxing Association freezes titles of those serving in armed services
Anti Jewish riot in Rome
Chicago Mayor Ed Kelly opens city's new subway system
Jewish quarter of Rome surrounded by Nazis, they are sent to Auschwitz
US 1st Army establishes headquarter in Bristol
Hungary's government of Miklós Horthy falls; Nazi German general Döme Sztójay becomes prime minister
UN's Food & Agriculture Organization comes into existence
WDAF TV channel 4 in Kansas City, MO (NBC) begins broadcasting
1st Prime Minister of Pakistan, Liaquat Ali Khan, is assassinated by Said Akbar in Rawalpindi
Pakistan's 1st Test starts, v India at Delhi
Woolworth's at Powell & Market (San Francisco) opens
USAF sends 2 aluminium bullets into space
Children's programme "Blue Peter", created by John Hunter Blair, premieres on the BBC
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
NL votes to admit Houston & NY to league
Byron R White becomes a Supreme Court Justice
KTXT TV channel 5 in Lubbock, TX (PBS) begins broadcasting
2 secret US military satellites launched from Cape Canaveral
NY newspaper "Mirror" last edition
American swimmer Sharon Strouder sets world record 1:04.7 to beat Ada Kok of the Netherlands by 0.9s and win the women's 100m butterfly at the Tokyo Olympics
China becomes world's 5th nuclear power

In an incredibly close women's 100m final at the Tokyo Olympics American sprinter Wyomia Tyus runs 11.4s to beat teammate Edith McGuire by 0.2s; Ewa Kłobukowska of Poland takes bronze with the same time as McGuire
Indians' directors vote to keep franchise in Cleveland, rejecting bids by Seattle, Oakland & Dallas
US men's 4 × 100m medley relay team of Thompson Mann, Bill Craig, Fred Schmidt & Steve Clark swim world record 3:58.4 to beat Germany by 3.2s and win gold medal at the Tokyo Olympics
13th General Conference on Weights and Measures in Paris ends after redefining the second using a cesium-beam atomic clock
Joean Baez and 123 other anti-draft protesters are arrested in Oakland, California
WETK TV channel 33 in Burlington, VT (PBS) begins broadcasting
WGNO TV channel 26 in New Orleans, LA (ABC) begins broadcasting
American Bob Seagren, Claus Schiprowski of West Germany and East German Wolfgang Nordwig all record 5.40m in the final of the pole vault at the Mexico City Olympics; Seagren awarded gold on countback
China reports removal of president Li Sjao-tji
Czechoslovakia & Russian "accord" rules allies Soviet forces
Jim Dorey sets Toronto Maple Leaf penalty records (48 mins on 9 penalties in a game & 44 minutes on 7 penalties in a period)
Milwaukee Bucks play their 1st game losing 89-84 to Chicago Bulls
The People's Democracy (PD), formed on Oct 9, organise a march of 1,300 students from the Queen's University of Belfast to the City Hall in the centre of the city, Northern Ireland
Amphitheater in McLaren Park is dedicated in San Francisco
2 members of the Official Irish Republican Army are shot dead by the British Army in County Tyrone
A Protestant youth member (15) of the Ulster Defence Association, and a UDA member (26) are run over by British Army vehicles during riots in east Belfast
Israeli tanks under Gen Sharon move through Suez Canal
Maynard Jackson elected 1st black mayor of Atlanta
Monks Heng Yo & Heng Ju, start 1000 mile SF to Seattle pilgrimage
The Gulf Six (Iran, Iraq, Abu Dhabi, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and Qatar) unilaterally raise the posted price of Saudi Light marker crude-oil by 17 percent
Soyuz 23 returns to Earth
Toronto Maple Leaf Lanny McDonald scores a hat trick in 2 min 54 sec
First global telenovela Mexican "Los ricos también lloran" (The Rich Also Cry) premieres, second foreign series to be screened in the Soviet Union
Nobel Prize for Economics awarded to Theodore Schultz and William Arthur Lewis for economic development research, particularly for developing countries
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
2nd Dutch government of Van Agt resigns
Harvey Fierstein's "Torch Song Trilogy," premieres in NYC
Mt Palomar Observatory 1st to detect Halley's comet on 13th return
USSR performs underground nuclear test
Challenger vehicle moves to launch pad for STS 61A mission
KC Royals & St Louis Cardinals win their league championships
Nobel prize for chemistry awarded to Herbert Hauptman & Jerome Karle
Armand Hammer returns to US with Jewish refusenik David Goldfarb
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
18-month-year-old Jessica McClure is rescued, 58 hrs after falling 22' into a well shaft in Midland, Texas
A record 338,500,000 shares are traded on the New York  stock exchange
Dow Jones Industrial Average falls more than 100 points for the 1st time (108.35)
Great Storm of 1987: 175-kph hurricane force winds hit London and much of the South of England killing 23 people and causing widespread blackouts
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
"Smile Jamaica" concert for Hurricane Gilbert victims held in London
Bikenibau Paeniu installed as premier of Tuvalu
Jan Syse becomes premier of Norway
Reds beat A's 7-0, ending Oakland's 10-game post-season winning streak
US forces reach 200,000 in Persian Gulf
Dallas Mavericks Roy Tarpley becomes 7th to be banned from NBA for life under the league's anti-drug agreement
George Jo Hennard, 35, kills 23 & himself & wounds 20 in Texas
Jharkhand Chhatra Yuva Morcha is founded at a conference in Ranchi, India.
US Supreme Court begins to hear Joseph Doherty case
Anti-Nazi riot breaks out in Welling in Kent, after police stop protesters approaching British National Party headquarters
IRA bomb attack on fish & chips restaurant in Belfast, 10 killed
"Addams Family" actor Raul Julia, actor suffers a stroke
Shreveport Pirates win first Canadian Football League game, 24-12 at home to Sacramento Gold Miners; fold 1995
Allan Donald takes 8-71 as South Africa defeat Zimbabwe
Million Man March held in Washington, D.C. (over 830,000 African American men attend)
ODI in Sharjah WI 7-333 in 50 overs beat Sri Lanka 329 all out
Eighty-four people are killed and more than 180 injured as 47,000 football fans attempt to squeeze into the 36,000-seat Estadio Mateo Flores in Guatemala City.

The US Coast Guard lifts a ban on liquefied natural gas (LNG) tankers entering Boston Harbor to make deliveries to Distrigas' Everett LNG terminal that had been imposed on September 26 in response to the terrorist attacks of September 11
Bibliotheca Alexandrina in the Egyptian city of Alexandria, a commemoration of the Library of Alexandria that was lost in antiquity, is officially inaugurated.
Conflict in Maiduguri, Nigeria, leads to 24 militant deaths and several structures set ablaze
18 people are killed after Typhoon Wipha strikes Japan
21 people are killed after a minibus hits a land mine in Nawa, Syria
49 people are killed after Lao Airlines Flight 301 crashes in the Mekong River, Laos
The United States ends its 16-day government shut down and avoids default in a Bi-partisan deal in the Senate
New Zealand, Malaysia, Angola, Spain and Venezuela are elected to the United Nations Security Council
Ed Whitlock (85) becomes oldest person to complete a marathon under 4 hours, at Toronto Waterfront Marathon in 3 hours 56 minutes

Findings are published of a neutron star collision that occurred two months prior on August 17, the first cosmic event observed in both gravitational waves and light, confirming that heavy elements such as gold result from such collisions
Iraqi army seizes control of oil-rich Kirkuk, from Kurdish peshmerga
Panama Papers Maltese journalist Daphne Caruana Galizia killed in a suspicious car bomb in Malta
Storm formed from Hurricane Ophelia lashes Ireland, killing 3 people
Chairman of China's Xinjiang’s government defends its detention camps for Uighur Muslims saying they provided “vocational education and training”
Man Booker Prize is won by Anna Burns' "Milkman", the first winner from Northern Ireland
Egyptian archaeologists announce discovery of more than 20 painted wooden coffins from Theban necropolis of Asasif
First plant-powered selfie taken by Pete the Fern as part of research into use of microbial fuel cells at London Zoo (London, England)
French teacher Samuel Paty beheaded by 18-year-old Islamist militant in Paris suburb of Éragny
NASA probe Lucy launched on mission to fly-by eight Trojan asteroids circling the sun
More than 600 people have died in Nigeria's worst floods in a decade, displacing more than 1.3 million people according to a government minister
Amazon's largest tributary, The Negro River, records its lowest-ever level, confirming the rainforest is in the midst of its most significant drought
Model of a Star Wars X-wing Starfighter used in a "Star Wars" film sells for over $3 million from collection of Hollywood model maker Greg Jein
Italian fashion house Prada and aerospace company Axiom Space unveil the new spacesuit for NASA's Artemis III mission to the moon in Milan
Christopher Columbus' fleet anchors at "Fernandina" (Long Island, Bahamas)
English demand the surrender of Quebec, French GovernorLouis de Buade de Frontenacreplies “I have no reply to make to your general other than from the mouths of my cannon and muskets”
Battle of Leipzig, the largest battle in Europe prior to World War I, seesNapoleon's forces defeated by Prussia, Austria, and Russia
Abraham Lincolnpresents his Peoria Speech, denouncing recent federal legislation extending slavery, on the lawn of the Peoria County Courthouse in Peoria, Illinois
AbolitionistJohn Brownleads 21 men on a raid of the federal arsenal at Harpers Ferry, Virginia
Brigham YoungUniversity is founded in Provo, Utah.
Booker T. Washingtonand his family are invited to dine at the White House with Teddy andEdith Roosevelt, prompting condemnation from the South
In his 4th title defenseJack JohnsonKOs Stanley Ketchel in the 12th round at Mission St Arena, Colma, California to retain his heavyweight boxing crown
Arnold Schoenberg's "Pierrot Lunaire," premieres at the Berlin Choralion-Saal sung by Albertine Zehme
George Bernard Shaw's play "Pygmalion" premieres in Hofburg Theatre in Vienna, Austria
Margaret Sangeropens 1st birth control clinic in the US at 46 Amboy St, Brooklyn
T. E. Lawrence(of Arabia) meets with Faisal I of Iraq
Influential modernist poem "The Waste Land" byT. S. Eliotis first published in the UK in magazine "The Criterion"
Mickey Cochranewins AL MVP honors, edging Heinie Manush by 2 points
Mao Zedongand 25,000 troops begin their 6,000 mile Long March from the south of China to the north and west
Jean Battenreaches Auckland, New Zealand after flying solo from Kent, England in a record 11 days and 45 minutes
Lou Gehrig, is voted AL MVP by BBWAA
Aaron Copland's & Eugene Loring's ballet "Billy the Kid" premieres in Chicago
Warsaw Ghetto is formed by German Governor-GeneralHans Frank
Romanian Legionnaires enter Odessa, Russia during thesiege of Odessa, part of the Axis invasion of the Soviet Union
Demonstration by Moscow Jews honoring Israeli ambassadorGolda Meir
The first edition ofC.S. Lewis' "The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe" is released in London
Fidel Castrosentenced to 15 years (Havana)
British Queen Elizabeth & her husbandPrince Philipvisit Williamsburg Virginia
Benjamin Britten's "Nocturne," premieres
Harold Wilson's Labour party wins British election
New York Yankees fire managerYogi Berra, one day after losing the World Series
Soyuz 6returns to Earth
Pierre Trudeauinvokes the War Measures Act as a response to the October Crisis, the only peacetime use of the War Measures Act in Canadian history.
American rock bandCreedence ClearwaterRevival break up
"The Way We Were", directed bySydney Pollackand starringBarbra StreisandandRobert Redford, premieres in New York City
US Secretary of StateHenry Kissingerand Le Duc Tho controversially awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for negotiating a ceasefire in Vietnam that later failed
Nobel Prize in Economics awarded to American political scientistHerbert A. Simon
Polish Cardinal Karol Wojtyla is elected PopeJohn Paul II
Test debut ofKapil Dev, India v Pakistan at Faisalabad
Comedy sketch show "Not the Nine O'Clock News" debuts starringRowan Atkinson, Chris Langham, Pamela Stephenson and Mel Smith on BBC 2
Secretary of StateGeorge P. Shultzwarns the US will withdraw from the UN if they vote to exclude Israel
Desmond Tutu, South African Anglican Archbishop, wins Nobel Peace Prize
Intelintroduces 32-bit 80386 microcomputer chip

Rolling Stones guitaristKeith Richardsorganizes concert celebrating rock and roll legendChuck Berry's 60th birthday at the Fox Theatre in St. Louis, Missouri; performers include: Richards, Berry, Johnnie Johnson,Eric Clapton,Etta James,Linda Ronstadt, Robert Cray, and Julian Lennon among others
US government shuts down due to disputes betweenPresident Reaganand the House
Wole Soyinka, Nigerian playwright and poet, becomes the first African to receive the Nobel Prize in Literature
Defending championMike Tysonbeats Tyrell Biggs by TKO in round 7 at Convention Center, Atlantic City, New Jersey; retains unified heavyweight boxing title
Warner Bros, Records releases "The Rhythm of the Saints", the eighth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon
"Gilligan's Island" TV pilot, filmed in 1963, is first shown on TV (TBS)
1,700thDavid LettermanShow
GeneralOmar al-Bashirappointed Sudan president
Brian Larascores 169 in Sharjah ODI versus Sri Lanka
Former Chilean dictator GeneralAugusto Pinochetis arrested in London on a Spanish warrant requesting his extradition on murder charges
"28 Days Later", directed byDanny Boyleand starring Cillian Murphy, premieres in London
17-year oldLionel Messimakes his league debut for FC Barcelona in a 1-0 win against cross-town rivals Espanyol at the Estadi Olímpic Lluís Companys, Barcelona
Renault driverFernando Alonsowins season-ending Chinese Grand Prix at Shanghai International Circuit; first Spanish Formula 1 World Drivers champion; wins by 21 points fromKimi Räikkönen
Britain'sPrince HarryandMeghan, Duchess of Sussexbegin their first royal tour visiting Australia and New Zealand
Netflix reveals its most popular original movie wasSandra Bullock's "Bird Box" and TV series "Stranger Things" for the year
Camden County Commissioners unveil Statue of boxing championJersey Joe Walcottat Wiggins Waterfront Park in Camden, New Jersey
Francis Drakesights land in the Philippines after crossing the Pacific Ocean aboard the 'Golden Hind'
Alaska adopts Gregorian calendar, crosses international date line
10 Nazi leaders are hanged as war criminals after Nuremberg war trials, including Wilhelm Keitel, Joachim von Ribbentrop andAlfred Jodl
Cuban Missile Crisis begins as US PresidentJohn F. Kennedyis shown photos confirming the presence of Soviet missiles in Cuba

AmericansTommie Smith(gold 19.83 WR) and John Carlos (bronze) famously give the Black Power salute on the 200m medal podium during the Mexico City Olympics to protest racism and injustice against African Americans
William de la Pole, 1st Duke of Suffolk, known as Jackanapes, English statesman under Henry VI and military commander in the Hundred Years War, born in Cotton, Suffolk (d. 1450)
King James II of Scotland (1437-60), born in Holyrood Abbey, Edinburgh (d. 1460)
Gasparo Contarini, Italian diplomat and cardinal who advocated for church reform, born in Venice (d. 1542)
Niwa Nagahide, Japanese samurai for the Oda clan, born in Nishi-ku, Nagoya (d. 1585)
Charles Coypeau Dassoucy, French poet and singer, born in Paris (d. 1677)
Pierre Paul Puget, French artist, born in Marseille, France (d. 1694)
Jan Dismas Zelenka, Bohemian composer, born in Louňovice, Lands of the Bohemian Crown (d. 1745)
Albrecht von Haller, Swiss experimental physiologist, biologist and author (Acadamy of Science), born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 1777)
Andreas Hadik, Austro-Hungarian general for the Holy Roman Empire, born in Csallóköz or Kőszeg, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1790)
Giovanni Arduino, Italian geologist who established 1st  system of stratigraphic chronology, born in Caprino Veronese, Republic of Venice (d. 1795)
Daniel Chodowiecki, German painter and printer, born in Danzig (d. 1801)
(Pieter) Pierre van Maldere, Flemish violinist and composer, born in Brussels, Southern Low Countries (d. 1768)
Frederica Louisa of Hesse-Darmstadt, Queen of Prussia as 2nd wife of King Frederick William II, who was obliged to accept her husband's bigamy, born in Prenzlau, Brandenburg (d. 1805)
Morgan Lewis, 3rd Governor of New York (1804-07), born in New York City (d. 1844)
Paul Hamilton, American politician, Governor of South Carolina (1804-06) and Secretary of the Navy (1809-12), born in Saint Paul's Parish, South Carolina (d. 1816)
Fédéric-Nicolas Duvernoy, French horn player and composer, born in Montbéliard, County of Montbéliard (d. 1838)
William Burton, American politician, 39th Governor of Delaware (1859-63), born in Indian River Hundred, Delaware (d. 1866)
William Buell Sprague, American clergyman and author who compiled "Annals of the American Pulpit ", born in Andover, Connecticut (d. 1876)
James Brudenell, British Earl Of Cardigan (led the Charge of the Light Brigade), born in Hambleden, England (d. 1868)
Isaac Murphy, 8th Governor of Arkansas (1864-68) who refused to vote for succession, born near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1882)
William Pitt Fessenden, American politician, Secretary of the Treasury during Civil War (Union), born in Boscawen, New Hampshire (d. 1869)
Francesco Schira, composer, born in Island of Malta (d. 1883)
Gaetano Capocci, Italian organist and composer (Responsori), born in Rome (d. 1898)
Francis Lubbock, 9th Governor of Texas during the Civil War, born in Beaufort, South Carolina (d. 1905)
William Preston, American lawyer, politician and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1887)
Austin F. Pike, American politician (Rep and Senator for New Hampshire), born in Hebron, New Hampshire (d. 1886)
Albert Franz Doppler, Austrian flautist and composer, born in Lemberg, Austrian Empire (d. 1883)
Thomas Turpin Crittenden, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Huntsville, Alabama (d. 1905)
Piotr Studzinski, Polish conductor and composer, born in the Free City of Cracow (d. 1869)
Arnold Böcklin, Swiss painter (Isle of the Dead), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1916)
Ferdinand van der Haeghen, Belgian librarian and bibliographer, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1913)
George Crockett Strong, American Major General (Union Army), born in Stockbridge, Vermont (d. 1863)
Vicente Riva Palacio, Mexican Liberal politician, military leader and writer (Governor of the State of Mexico), born in Mexico City (d. 1896)
John Francis Barnett, British composer, born in London (d. 1916)
Maria Pia of Savoy, Queen of Portugal (1862-89), born in the Royal Palace, Turin, Sardinia (d. 1911)
Arnold Krug, German composer, born in Hamburg, German Confederation (d. 1904)
Charles Harford Lloyd, British organist and composer, born in Thornbury, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1919)
James A. Ten Eyck, American champion rower and coach (Ten Eyck Trophy namesake), born in Tompkins Cove, New York (d. 1938)
Karl Kautsky, Austrian philosopher and Marxist theorist (Socialist journal Neue Zeit), born in Prague, Austria-Hungary (d. 1938)
Camille Looten, French=Flemish priest and literature historian (Flemish committee of France), born in Noordpeene, Belgium (d. 1944)
Samedbey Mehmandarov, Azerbaijani Russian general during WWI, born in Lankaran, Azerbaijan (d. 1931)
Henryk Pachulski, Polish pianist, composer, and educator (Moscow Conservatory, 1886-1917), born in Łazy, Poland (d. 1921)
J. B. Bury, Irish historian, born in County Monaghan, Ireland (d. 1927)
Austen Chamberlain, British statesman, (Foreign Secretary 1924-29, Nobel Peace Prize 1925 for anti-war Locano Pact), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1937)
Franz Ritter von Epp, German general and Nazi official (Reichsstatthalter for Bavaria 1933-45), born in Munich, Bavaria (d. 1947)
Helge Rode, Danish poet and critic, born in Frederiksberg, Denmark (d. 1937)
Carlos Pedrell, Uruguayan guitarist and composer, born in Minas, Uraguay (d. 1941)
Maxie Long, American athlete (Olympic gold 400m 1900), born in Waverley, Massachusetts (d. 1959)
Vasiliki Maliaros, Greek-American actress (The Exorcist), born in Athens, Greece (d. 1973)
Will Harridge, American Baseball HOF executive (President American League 1931-59), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1971)
Rembrandt Bugatti, Italian sculptor known for bronze wildlife work, born in Milan, Italy- (d. 1916)
Dorando Pietri, Italian athlete (Olympic-famous DQ gold marathon 1908), born in Correggio, Italy (d. 1942)
Armin T. Wegner, German medic, photographer, writer, and civil rights activist (documented the Armenian Genocide), born in Eberfeld, Germany (d. 1978)[1]
Paul Popenoe, American marriage counsellor, eugenicist who advocated for sterilization of mentally impaired people, born in Topeka, Kansas (d. 1979)
Maria Goretti, Italian saint who died aged 11, born in Corinaldo, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1902)
Paul Strand, American photographer (Native Land-1942), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1976)
Harrison Kerr, American composer, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1978)
Louis de Cazenave, France's oldest living man (oldest survivor from WWI), born in Saint-Georges-d'Aurac, France (d. 2008)
Arthur H. Dean, American lawyer, ambassador and presidential advisor (1963 nuclear test ban treaty), born in Ithaca, New York (d. 1987)[1]
William O. Douglas, American 81st Supreme Court justice (1939-75), born in Maine (d. 1980)
Edward Ardizzone, British war artist and illustrator known for "Tim All Alone", born in Haiphong, Tonkin (d. 1979)
Goose Goslin, American Baseball HOF left fielder (World Series 1924 Washington Sens, 1935 Detroit Tigers; AL batting champion 1928; MLB All Star 1936), born in Salem, New Jersey (d. 1971)
Lloyd Corrigan, American actor (Papa Dodger, The Manchurian Candidate), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1969)
Primo Conti, Italian futurist painter, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1988)
Cecile de Brunhoff, French storyteller who created the Barbar story, born in Paris (d. 2003)
Rex Bell [George Francis Beldam], American cowboy actor (Cowboys & Injuns) and 21st Lieutenant Governor of Nevada, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1962)
Reginald Dixon, British theatre organist, born in Sheffield, Britain, (d. 1985)
Wild Bill Elliott [Gordon Nance], American actor (Beyond the Sacramento), born in Pattonsburg, Missouri (d. 1965)
Dino Buzzati, Italian writer (The Tartar Steppe), born in San Pellegrino, Belluno, (d. 1972)
George Lott, American tennis player (12 Grand Slam doubles titles), born in Springfield, Illinois (d. 1991)
Roger Vailland, French author (Drôle de jeu, La loi) and screenwriter, born in Acy-en-Multien, Oise, France (d. 1965)
William Leonard "Will" Reed, British pianist and composer, born in South London (d. 2002)
Cesar Bresgen, Austrian organist and composer, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1988)
George Turner, Australian author known for "The Sea and Summer", born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1997)
Alice Pearce, American comedienne and actress (On the Town, Bewitched), born in New York City (d. 1966)
Bill Nichols, American politician (Rep-D-AL, 1967-88), born in Monroe County, Mississippi (d. 1988)
Géori Boué, French soprano, born in Toulouse, France (d. 2017)
Tony Rolt, English auto racer (24 Hours of Le Mans, 1953; British Empire Trophy, 1939), born in Bordon, England (d. 2008)
Kathleen Winsor, American writer known for "Forever Amber", born in Olivia, Minnesota (d. 2003)
Matt Batts, MLB catcher (Red Sox), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 2013)
Michael Conrad, American Emmy Award-winning actor (Hill Street Blues - "Sgt. Phil Esterhaus"), born in New York City (d. 1983)
Max Bygraves [Walter William Bygraves], British actor (Charley Moon, Spare the Rod, Tom Brown's School Days), born in London (d. 2012)
Robert Urquhart, Scottish actor (Dunkirk, Bulldog Breed, Dark Avenger), born in Ullapool, Scotland (d. 1995)
Bert Kaempfert, German orchestra leader, songwriter ("Strangers in the Night"; "Danke Schoen"), and record producer (The Beatles first recordings), born in Hamburg (d. 1980)
Linda Darnell, American actress (Unfaithfully Yours, A Letter to Three Wives), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1965)
Daniel J. Evans, American politician (Senator-R-Washington, 1983-89, Governor of Washington 1965-77), born in Seattle, Washington
Hansheinz Schneeberger, Swiss violinist (Duo Paganini), born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 2019)
Gus Yatron, American boxer and politician (Rep-D-PA, 1969-93), born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 2003)
Lee Montague [Leonard Goldberg], British stage and screen actor (Holocaust - "Uncle Sasha"; Jackanory), born in Bow, London (d. 2025)
Ann Morgan Guilbert, American actress (The Dick Van Dyke Show - "Millie Helper"; The Nanny - "Yetta"), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2016)
Mary Daly, American theologian and feminist scholar, born in Schenectady, New York (d. 2010)
B. Satyaji Rao, Indian cricket umpire (17 Tests 1960-79; 5 x Ranji Trophy finals), born in Bangalore, India (d. 2021)
Bill Green, American politician (Rep-R-NY, 1978-93), born in New York City (d. 2002)
Fernanda Montenegro [Arlette Torres], Brazilian actress, often described as the greatest Brazilian actress of all time (Central Station; Coce de Mãe), born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
Walt Michaels, American NFL linebacker, coach (5-time Pro Bowl; Cleveland Browns; head coach NY Jets 1977-82), born in Swoyersville, Pennsylvania (d. 2019)
Carmen Sevilla [María del Carmen García], Spanish actress, singer and TV presenter (Vengeance; Searching for Monica; Don Juan), born in Seville, Spain (d. 2023)
Charles W Colson, presidential adviser (Watergate figure), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2012)

John Ronayne, Irish classical and session violinist (Radio Éireann Symphony Orchestra; London Philharmonic; Royal Philharmonic; The Beatles - "All You Need is Love. "Yellow Submarine"), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 2009)
Rosa Rosal [Florence Gayda], Filipino actress and humanitarian described as "original femme fatale of Philippine cinema", born in Manila, Philippine Islands
Henry Jay Lewis, American double-bassist and conductor (Los Angeles Philharmonic, 1948-65), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1996)
John Grant, British Labour and SDP politician, born in London (d. 2000)
Rolf Geiger, German soccer forward (8 caps; VfB Stuttgart 186 games, 78 goals), born in Marbach am Neckar, Germany (d. 2023)
Sugar Pie DeSanto [Umpeylia Balinton], Filipino-American R&B singer-songwriter ("Soulful Dress", "In the Basement"), and dancer, born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2024)[1][2]
Akira Machida, Japanese judge, Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Japan, born in Shimonoseki, Japan (d. 2015)
